column2: definition
column4: example

column2: A group of organs that work together to carry out a shared function.
column4: The eleven organ systems are the building blocks of human anatomy.

column2: The body's ability to keep its internal conditions stable despite changes outside.
column4: Homeostasis breaks down when the body can't cool itself during heatstroke.

column2: A control loop that reverses a change to bring a value back to its set point.
column4: Shivering is a negative-feedback response to a drop in core temperature.

column2: A control loop that amplifies a change rather than reversing it.
column4: Uterine contractions during childbirth use positive feedback.

column2: The organ system comprising skin
column4: and nails.
column6: The integumentary system is the body's first line of defense against infection.

column2: The outer layer of the skin.
column4: The epidermis thickens where the skin takes repeated pressure, forming calluses.

column2: The middle layer of the skin containing blood vessels
column4: and glands.
column6: A paper cut reaches the dermis when it starts to bleed.

column2: The process of maintaining core body temperature.
column4: Sweating is the body's main thermoregulation strategy in the heat.

column2: The organ system of bones
column4: and cartilage.
column6: The skeletal system does more than hold you up — it also stores calcium.

column2: The ongoing process of breaking down and rebuilding bone tissue.
column4: Bone remodeling is why a fracture can heal and why bones weaken without use.

column2: A condition in which bones become weak and brittle due to lost density.
column4: Osteoporosis raises the risk of hip fracture after age 65.

column2: The organ system of skeletal
column4: and cardiac muscle.
column6: The muscular system generates movement, posture, and body heat.

column2: Voluntary muscle attached to bone that produces movement.
column4: Skeletal muscle is what you flex when you lift a backpack.

column2: Involuntary muscle found in organ walls and blood vessels.
column4: Smooth muscle pushes food through the digestive tract.

column2: Involuntary striated muscle found only in the heart.
column4: Cardiac muscle contracts about 100,000 times a day without fatiguing.

column2: The organ system of the heart
column4: and blood vessels.
column6: The cardiovascular system delivers oxygen to every cell and carries waste away.

column2: The volume of blood the heart pumps per minute.
column4: Cardiac output rises during exercise to meet muscle demand.

column2: A blood vessel that carries blood away from the heart.
column4: Arteries have thick walls to handle the pressure of each heartbeat.

column2: A blood vessel that carries blood back to the heart.
column4: Veins have valves to keep blood from flowing backward.

column2: The smallest blood vessel
column3: where gases and nutrients exchange with tissues.
column4: noun
column5: Oxygen leaves a capillary and diffuses into surrounding muscle cells.

column2: Chronically high blood pressure.
column4: Untreated hypertension damages the heart, kidneys, and eyes over years.

column2: The organ system of lymph vessels
column4: and immune organs.
column6: The lymphatic system returns leaked fluid to the bloodstream and fights infection.

column2: A clear fluid that flows through lymph vessels carrying immune cells.
column4: Lymph drains from tissue into nearby lymph nodes to be screened for pathogens.

column2: The body's coordinated defense against pathogens.
column4: A fever is part of the immune response, not a malfunction of it.

column2: The organ system of the airways and lungs.
column4: The respiratory system moves oxygen into the blood and carbon dioxide out.

column2: The swap of oxygen and carbon dioxide between air and blood in the lungs.
column4: Gas exchange happens across the thin walls of the alveoli.

column2: A tiny air sac in the lung where gas exchange occurs.
column4: Each lung holds roughly 300 million alveoli.

column2: The organ system that breaks down food and absorbs nutrients.
column4: The digestive system stretches from the mouth to the anus.

column2: Wave-like muscle contractions that move contents through the GI tract.
column4: Peristalsis pushes a swallowed bite through the esophagus in about eight seconds.

column2: The movement of digested nutrients from the gut into the blood.
column4: Most absorption happens in the small intestine.

column2: The organ system of kidneys
column4: bladder
column7: The urinary system filters blood and regulates fluid balance.

column2: The filtering unit of the kidney.
column4: Each kidney contains about a million nephrons.

column2: The volume of plasma filtered by the kidneys per minute.
column4: A falling glomerular filtration rate is a key sign of chronic kidney disease.

column2: Long-term loss of kidney function.
column4: Chronic kidney disease often progresses silently until late stages.

column2: The organ system of hormone-producing glands.
column4: The endocrine system regulates growth, metabolism, mood, and reproduction.

column2: A chemical messenger released into the bloodstream by an endocrine gland.
column4: Insulin is the hormone that lets cells take up glucose after a meal.

column2: A hormone from the pancreas that lowers blood glucose.
column4: People with type 1 diabetes must inject insulin to replace what their pancreas can't make.

column2: A group of conditions marked by high blood glucose.
column4: Diabetes mellitus raises the risk of kidney, nerve, eye, and heart disease.

column2: The organ system of the brain
column4: and peripheral nerves.
column6: The nervous system carries electrical signals that coordinate every other system.

column2: The brain and spinal cord.
column4: The central nervous system processes information and issues commands.

column2: The nerves outside the brain and spinal cord.
column4: The peripheral nervous system carries signals between the CNS and the rest of the body.

column2: A nerve cell that transmits electrical signals.
column4: A single motor neuron can reach from the spinal cord all the way to a toe muscle.

column2: The organ system of gonads and accessory structures that produce offspring.
column4: The reproductive system also releases hormones that shape puberty.

column2: An organ that produces gametes and sex hormones (ovary or testis).
column4: The gonads are both reproductive organs and endocrine glands.

column2: A structure made of two or more tissues that work together for a function.
column4: The heart is an organ made of muscle, connective, and nervous tissue.

column2: A group of similar cells that perform a shared function.
column4: Muscle tissue is one of the four basic tissue types in the body.

column2: The value a feedback loop tries to hold a variable near.
column4: The set point for human core temperature is about 37 degrees Celsius.

column2: A control system that senses a value and triggers a response when it drifts.
column4: Blood glucose is managed by a feedback loop involving the pancreas and liver.

column2: A pressure sensor in blood-vessel walls that helps regulate blood pressure.
column4: When baroreceptors detect a pressure drop, the nervous system speeds up the heart.

column2: Standard measurements of body function: temperature
column4: respiration
column8: Vital signs are the first data point collected at almost every patient visit.

column2: The pressure wave you can feel each time the heart beats.
column4: A nurse takes a pulse at the radial artery for 30 seconds and doubles it.

column2: The force blood exerts on artery walls
column4: compound
column5: A reading of 120 over 80 millimeters of mercury is considered normal blood pressure.

column2: A process that requires two or more organ systems to work together.
column4: Regulating blood pressure is a system interaction between cardiovascular, renal, endocrine, and nervous systems.

column2: The coordinated change in heart rate and breathing rate during exertion or stress.
column4: Cardiorespiratory coupling is why your heart and breath both speed up when you sprint.

column2: A disease in which the immune system attacks the body's own tissue.
column4: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune disease that destroys pancreatic beta cells.

column2: Brain injury from loss of blood flow (ischemic) or bleeding (hemorrhagic).
column4: A stroke can damage multiple systems because the brain controls them all.

column2: Any condition that disrupts the normal function of a system.
column4: A disorder in one system often strains others — kidney disease raises blood pressure.
